description: Symptom: Customers see frequent log entries: HTTP <MAC-ADDRESS> - Invalid packet length or headers during normal HTTP traffic, even when no real packet corruption exists. No functional impact; just log noise. Scenario: This issue occurs on legitimate TCP packets to port 80 with no payload (ACK, SYN/ACK, FIN, keep-alive, window updates). Previously these were funneled into an error path (or indistinguishable from malformed cases), producing repeated ERR logs under moderate web or update traffic. Workaround: Disable the HTTP user-agent in device fingerprinting configuration.

description: Symptom: Older log files are not removed during log rotation. Scenario: When the maximum log file size is reached, newly created logs fail to be added to the chunk files. As a result, older chunk log files become stale and are not deleted as expected during log rotation. Workaround: Manually remove the older chunk log files before the log file reaches its maximum size to prevent stale files from accumulating.

description: Symptom: Older log files are not removed during log rotation. Scenario: When the maximum log file size is reached, newly created logs fail to be added to the chunk files. As a result, older chunk log files become stale and are not deleted as expected during log rotation. Workaround: Manually remove the older chunk log files before the log file reaches its maximum size to prevent stale files from accumulating.

description: Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the swith, switch #1's port 1/1/2 will be assigned the identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two 6200, 6300, 6300L, 6400, 8100, or 8360 switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or don't use both of the ports with the overlap. Neither of those options is a feasible solution for a customer. Prior to the fix provided here, there is no explicit workaround for this issue.

description: Symptom: When wireless clients roam between access points connected to the same switch, they lose connectivity and receive an APIPA address. Connectivity is only restored after the MAC-age timer expires. Scenario: When a client is authenticated with a VLAN on a secure port, the VLAN is added as an ingress-only VLAN on other secure ports, but the MSTP state for those ports remains in blocked state instead of forwarding state, preventing seamless client movement. Workaround: Reduce the MAC-age timer to minimize the impact. or configure AP ports in device-mode instead of client-mode: interface 1/1/X port-access client-limit 1 port-access client-mode device.

description: Symptom: Users do not receive multicast traffic due to missing PIM join messages and registration messages in a multicast environment. Scenario: This issue occurs when trying to establish multicast communication in a network with multiple switches. description: Symptom: Traffic loss in an SVI with vsx active-forwarding enabled, when vsx active-forwarding is disabled/removed on a entirely different SVI. Scenario: In a configuration where vsx active-forwarding is enabled, a special HW resource must be programmed for the VSX peers to handle traffic destined to the active-gateway IP. The trigger for this programming is tied to our router MAC programming (packets destined to this MAC that ingress on an L3 interface will perform an L3 lookup), and the ref counting for these router MACs had a bug. Since the ref counting would decrement early, we would remove our special HW resource and traffic loss can occur depending on which peer it lands on. Workaround: Unconfigure and reconfigure vsx active-forwarding in the SVI, or save the config and reboot the VSX peers

description: Symptom: The VSX daemon may become unresponsive or hang due to a deadlock condition. Workaround: Restarting the VSX daemon resolves the issue.

description: Symptom: Customers will observe continuous system reboots on the affected switch. The system enters a reboot loop, preventing normal network operation and management access. Scenario: This issue occurs in a specific VSF (Virtual Switching Framework) environment when the following conditions are met: Vxlan Tunnel Underlay routes are being resolved. Before the system could process the route resolution and program the tunnel, the commander went for a reboot. When the new commander came and when the system started processing the request, it programmed tunnel underlay with invalid information. The invalid configuration prevents the member from successfully joining the stack, triggering continuous reboots. Workaround: The following procedures will clear the incorrect status information. This should reprogram the tunnel properly. Flap the underlay connections Perform a shut and no shut operation on the VXLAN.

description: Symptom: After upgrading to the FL.10.17.0001BB image, all LAG (ISL) member interfaces remain stuck in the Initializing state if any LAG member belongs to a line card in down state. Scenario: Interfaces in initializing state cause ISL to be out of sync and therefore VSX is not functional, having only Primary peer LAGs in UP state. The issue is applicable to LAG and can be seen on reboot path or checkpoint restore if LAG member(s) belong to LC in down state. Workaround: Remove the member interface(s) belongs to LC in Down State from the LAG Config before doing image upgrade to 10.17.0001.

description: Symptom: A drop occurs when packet(s) match an applied policy with PBR default-nexthop action and the LPM/longest prefix matching route in the VRF for that packet is a route type that copies to CPU (unresolved, nullroute, reject or interface). Scenario: This issue can occur in the following workflows: A policy with PBR action is applied to a Layer 3 interface in a given VRF in the routed-in direction and the selected/active entry from the PBR action list is of type default-nexthop. A packet ingresses the system on the same Layer 3 interface and the packet is matched by the qualifiers in the policy's IPv4 or IPv6 class. There exists one or more matching routes for the packet's destination address in the VRF and the longest prefix matching route (LPM) of that set is one of the following route types: + unresolved - the route's configured IPv4/IPv6 nexthop is currently unreachable + interface - the route is configured to egress matching traffic directly to another Layer 3 interface in the VRF, e.g. ROP, VLAN SVI or L3 LAG + nullroute - the route is configured to discard packets and return ICMP error to the sender + reject - the route is configured to discard packets silently This situation could occur randomly as dynamic routing topology changes introduce or remove routes of varying prefix lengths and nexthop states and/or the highest priority entry in the PBR action list changes type (e.g. default-nexthop to nexthop) depending on the changing reachability of the list entries. Workaround: Avoid using the PBR default-nexthop action entirely and use nexthop action instead, taking into account the fact that nexthop has higher priority and will redirect over any routing table entries in the VRF where the default-nexthop action has priority over the VRF's default route only, if one exists.

A packet ingresses the system on the same Layer 3 interface and the packet is matched by the qualifiers in the policy's IPv4 or IPv6 class. There exists one or more matching routes for the packet's destination address in the VRF and the longest prefix matching route (LPM) of that set is one of the following route types: + unresolved - the route's configured IPv4/IPv6 nexthop is currently unreachable + interface - the route is configured to egress matching traffic directly to another Layer 3 interface in the VRF, e.g. ROP, VLAN SVI or L3 LAG + nullroute - the route is configured to discard packets and return ICMP error to the sender + reject - the route is configured to discard packets silently This situation could occur randomly as dynamic routing topology changes introduce or remove routes of varying prefix lengths and nexthop states and/or the highest priority entry in the PBR action list changes type (e.g. default-nexthop to nexthop) depending on the changing reachability of the list entries. Workaround: Avoid using the PBR default-nexthop action entirely and use nexthop action instead, taking into account the fact that nexthop has higher priority and will redirect over any routing table entries in the VRF where the default-nexthop action has priority over the VRF's default route only, if one exists.

description: Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the system, Switch #1's port 1/1/2 is assigned an identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.

description: Symptom: After upgrading to the FL.10.17.0001BB image, all LAG (ISL) member interfaces remain stuck in the Initializing state if any LAG member belongs to a line card in down state. Scenario: Interfaces in initializing state cause ISL to be out of sync and therefore VSX is not functional, having only Primary peer LAGs in UP state. The issue is applicable to LAG and can be seen on reboot path or checkpoint restore if LAG member(s) belong to LC in down state. Workaround: Remove the member interface(s) belongs to LC in Down State from the LAG Config before doing image upgrade to 10.17.0001.
